one_line_takeaway: CorruptRAG shows that a single, cheap, black-box poisoned text per query can hijack RAG outputs with 85-98% success and evade four defenses, making prior multi-text poisoning assumptions unnecessary and RAG knowledge integrity a real deployment risk.
key_finding: Under the realistic single-poisoned-text constraint, CorruptRAG dramatically outperforms all baselines: e.g., on NQ with GPT-4o-mini, CorruptRAG-AS reaches ASR 0.97 and CorruptRAG-AK 0.95, versus PoisonedRAG black-box 0.69, PoisonedRAG white-box 0.67, PIA 0.85, and CPA 0.02. Baselines like PoisonedRAG only recover strong performance when allowed multiple texts (5 texts/query on NQ gives black-box ASR 0.89, white-box 0.95), which the authors argue is impractical. The attack is essentially free (AS = $0.0000/query, AK ~ $0.0001/query). It resists all four evaluated defenses: paraphrasing (CorruptRAG stays 74-96% ASR), instructional prevention (unchanged ~89-98%, while PIA drops sharply), LLM-based detection (true-positive rate <=0.10, showing p^h_i is not treated as an explicit instruction), and even the authors' own stronger 'correct knowledge expansion' defense (which crushes PoisonedRAG to ~1% but leaves CorruptRAG above 70%).
core_contribution: The paper introduces CorruptRAG, a practical knowledge-database poisoning attack against Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) that injects only ONE poisoned text per targeted query, in contrast to prior attacks (notably PoisonedRAG) that require injecting enough poisoned texts to outnumber the correct-answer texts in the top-N retrieved set. The authors motivate the single-text constraint empirically: on Natural Questions, most queries have fewer than three truly relevant texts in the top-5, so prior multi-text attacks (e.g., 5 poisoned texts/query) are costly and easy to detect. They propose two variants: CorruptRAG-AS (a fixed adversarial template, zero LLM cost) and CorruptRAG-AK (an LLM-refined 'adversarial knowledge' version that generalizes to related queries). Both the constraint framing and the two attack variants are the paper's own contribution; PoisonedRAG, PIA, and CPA are cited baselines, and correct-knowledge-expansion is a new defense the authors themselves introduce.
threat_model: Targeted attack: for each query in a chosen set the attacker designates a specific desired (incorrect) answer and manipulates the knowledge database so the RAG system returns it. Attacker's knowledge is deliberately weak/practical: NO access to the knowledge-database texts, NO access to LLM parameters or ability to query the LLM directly, and a BLACK-BOX retriever (no access to retriever parameters). The attacker is assumed to know the correct answer for each targeted query, justified because it can be obtained by simply querying the RAG system beforehand. Capability: inject a small amount of poisoned text (here, exactly one text per query) into a public, user-editable knowledge source such as Wikipedia or Reddit, which the paper argues is realistic (citing Carlini et al. on practical web-scale poisoning).
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) experiments cover only closed-ended queries (definite answers, easy ASR measurement); open-ended queries are untested. (2) Only targeted attacks are considered; untargeted attacks affecting arbitrary queries are left for future work. (3) Transferability to other/multi-modal RAG systems is claimed but not demonstrated. Reviewer-noted scope caveats grounded in the text: evaluation uses a single retriever family plus two alternatives (Contriever/Contriever-ms/ANCE) and a single generator family (GPT-3.5/4o-mini/4o/4-turbo), with 100 queries per dataset; the 'attacker knows the correct answer' and 'public user-editable database' assumptions, while argued, bound applicability. The correct-knowledge-expansion defense still reduces CorruptRAG ASR meaningfully (e.g., NQ 0.97->0.80, HotpotQA 0.98->0.74), so it is not fully defense-proof.

datasets_used: MS-MARCO - Bing web docs, 8,841,823 texts, 6,980 queries (BEIR)
quantitative_results: NQ, GPT-4o-mini, single text: CorruptRAG-AS ASR 0.97, CorruptRAG-AK ASR 0.95 vs PoisonedRAG black-box 0.69, white-box 0.67, PIA 0.85, CPA 0.02
HotpotQA, GPT-4o-mini: CorruptRAG-AS ASR 0.98, AK 0.97 vs PoisonedRAG black-box 0.83, PIA 0.95, CPA 0.01
MS-MARCO, GPT-4o-mini: CorruptRAG-AS ASR 0.92, AK 0.96 vs PoisonedRAG black-box 0.69, PIA 0.87, CPA 0.10
PoisonedRAG on NQ recovers only with 5 texts/query: black-box ASR 0.89 (F1 0.96), white-box 0.95 (F1 1.00)
Clean (no-attack) RAG accuracy: 81% NQ, 80% HotpotQA, 84% MS-MARCO
Cost per poisoned query: CorruptRAG-AS $0.0000, CorruptRAG-AK ~$0.0001 (GPT-4o-mini pricing $0.15/1M input, $0.60/1M output tokens)
Paraphrasing defense (MS-MARCO): CorruptRAG-AS 0.92->0.74, AK 0.96->0.79; PoisonedRAG black-box 0.69->0.54
Instructional-prevention defense (GPT-4-turbo): CorruptRAG-AS NQ 0.94->0.94, PIA NQ 0.78->0.63 (defense hurts PIA, not CorruptRAG)
LLM-based detection: CorruptRAG TPR <=0.10 (0.00 on HotpotQA/MS-MARCO) with ASR retained ~0.92-0.98; PIA TPR 0.83-0.96 with ASR collapsing to ~0.05-0.06
Correct-knowledge-expansion defense (K=5 benign texts, N=10): PoisonedRAG black-box reduced to ~0.01-0.17; CorruptRAG stays >=0.72 (NQ AS 0.97->0.80, HotpotQA AS 0.98->0.74, MS-MARCO AK 0.96->0.77)

Robustness to N: ASR roughly stable as N increases from 5 to 30; concatenation order p^s(+)p^h beats p^h(+)p^s (reverse still >75% ASR); max attainable F1 with one poisoned text at N=5 is 0.33 (=2/(N+1))
